stories.title: KKR, AEW Seek Exits from Chinese Commercial Property Holdings
stories.excerpt: KKR and AEW Capital Management seek to sell commercial property holdings in China as global investors continue to retreat from the country’s prolonged real estate downturn, Bloomberg News reported. KKR is marketing nine properties across China, including a high-end apartment complex in suburban Beijing and a hotel on Shanghai’s historic Bund, while AEW is looking to sell several Beijing office assets and Shanghai’s Pudong Development Bank building (pictured). Sources told Bloomberg both firms expect any sales to garner enough to repay bank loans, implying valuations of roughly 50% to 60% of the original purchase prices. The planned divestments underscore mounting pressure on foreign real estate investors after years of falling property values and weak leasing demand in China. Overseas investors poured nearly $140 billion into Chinese commercial real estate over the past 15 years but have largely become net sellers amid an economic slowdown and oversupply, according to Bloomberg. The post KKR, AEW Seek Exits from Chinese Commercial Property Holdings appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: KKR, AEW marketing Chinese commercial assets at 50–60% of original cost.
Foreign investors net sellers; $140B poured into China RE over 15 years.
Prolonged oversupply and weak leasing demand driving global retreat from market.
stories.summary: KKR and AEW Capital are exiting Chinese commercial real estate holdings, marketing nine properties (including a Beijing apartment complex and Shanghai office tower) at deep discounts—roughly half their original purchase prices. Over the past 15 years, overseas investors deployed nearly $140 billion into Chinese commercial real estate but have shifted to net selling as the market faces prolonged oversupply, weak leasing demand, and economic slowdown. Both firms expect sales proceeds to cover only loan repayment, reflecting massive value destruction.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact to U.S. multifamily. However, the scale of capital withdrawal from China signals risk-off sentiment globally; this may tighten U.S. development financing as institutional capital previously allocated to China re-deploys defensively or remains sidelined, potentially compressing cap rates and increasing cost of capital for U.S. multifamily development in 2026–2027.

stories.title: Crescent Building 248-Unit Rental Property in N. Phoenix
stories.excerpt: Crescent Communities closed on a parcel of land for NOVEL Norterra, a new four-story multifamily community planned in northern Phoenix, Arizona. Situated on an 8.5-acre site just east of I-17 in the Norterra neighborhood, the development will include 248 for-rent residences near the TSMC Arizona semiconductor manufacturing complex, retail, and outdoor destinations. Construction is scheduled to begin in July 2026, with first move-ins anticipated in Spring 2028. NOVEL Norterra is a part of the Norterra neighborhood, a master plan of nearly 600 acres of commercial, retail, community, and residential uses. Planned amenities include a clubhouse, a pool courtyard anchored by a 1,822-square-foot pool and spa with cabanas and fire features, a fully shaded pickleball court and a dog park, along with additional outdoor cooking, dining, and gathering areas and landscaped spaces that encourage residents to spend time outside. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Crescent Building 248-Unit Rental Property in N. Phoenix appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Crescent closed land for 248-unit rental near TSMC Arizona semiconductor plant, North Phoenix.
Construction starts July 2026; first move-in Spring 2028; four-story garden-style.
Positioned in Norterra master plan (600 acres); anchored by tech employer proximity.
stories.summary: Crescent Communities acquired an 8.5-acre parcel in North Phoenix's Norterra neighborhood to develop NOVEL Norterra, a 248-unit multifamily community. The site sits directly east of I-17, minutes from TSMC Arizona's semiconductor manufacturing complex. Construction begins July 2026 with occupancy in Spring 2028; the project is part of a larger 600-acre mixed-use master plan and features resort-style amenities (pool, cabanas, pickleball, dog park).
stories.impact: TSMC's $12B Arizona fab (operational 2024–2025) is a structural demand driver for North Phoenix multifamily; Crescent is capturing upstream demand from fab workers, contractors, and supply-chain employees. Early entitlement and construction timing position NOVEL Norterra to absorb first wave of tech-sector in-migration. Watch for rent growth acceleration in Norterra/North Phoenix submarkets through 2028–2029 as fab ramps and supply chain settles; this validates TSMC-adjacent land as premium development sites for future acquisitions.

stories.title: Two Mesquite Retail Centers Trade for a Combined $45M
stories.excerpt: Blueprint Investment Properties purchased the 100%-leased Broadmoor Plaza in Mesquite. About the same time, Newport Capital Partners purchased the Towne Crossing development.(shown). The Dallas Business Journal reports that David Disney of Disney Investment Group, who helped facilitate both deals, said both deals had a combined value of $45 million. Broadmoor was sold by Dogwood Commercial. Towne Crossing was previously owned by Phillips Edison & Co., a publicly traded real estate investment trust that is one of the largest owners and operators of grocery-anchored shopping centers. The 86,302-square-foot Broadmoor Plaza recently underwent a renovation and is anchored by a Dollar Tree, Five Below, Hibbet Sports and various other regional and national tenants. Towne Crossing, a 167,957-square-foot development, is anchored by a Kroger and 92% leased. The shopping center features 31 tenants including Hertz, Citi Trends and other regional tenants. In addition to Disney, Adam Crockett of Disney Investment Group represented the sellers in both deals. The post Two Mesquite Retail Centers Trade for a Combined $45M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Two Mesquite retail centers traded for combined $45M; both grocery-anchored, 86–168k SF.
Broadmoor Plaza (100% leased, renovated) and Towne Crossing (92% leased, Kroger anchor).
Investor transitions: Phillips Edison (REIT) sold; Blueprint, Newport Capital acquired.
stories.summary: Two retail centers in Mesquite, Texas—Broadmoor Plaza (86,302 SF, 100% leased) and Towne Crossing (167,957 SF, 92% leased)—traded hands for a combined $45 million. Both are grocery-anchored; Broadmoor features Dollar Tree and Five Below, while Towne Crossing is anchored by Kroger. Phillips Edison, a major REIT grocery-anchor owner, divested Towne Crossing, while Blueprint Investment Properties acquired the recently renovated Broadmoor.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily relevance. However, REIT divestitures of stabilized retail suggest capital reallocation; Phillips Edison's divestiture may signal tightening of grocery-retail returns or portfolio optimization, which could redirect capital toward higher-yielding asset classes. Mesquite's retail fundamentals remain solid (high occupancy, grocery-backed); proximity to Dallas MSA makes this an indirect indicator of demand strength in the Metroplex. Multifamily developers should monitor whether retail REITs continue opportunistic sales—could signal broader repricing or refinancing pressure in commercial real estate.

stories.title: Pfizer Incident or Not, New York Still Has a Lot of Office Conversions Left to Go
stories.excerpt: For a few days last week, two buckling steel columns at the Pfizer Building became the biggest real estate story in America. If you only read the headlines, you might conclude that office-to-residential conversions are suddenly in trouble. The opposite is closer to the truth. Quietly, New York has become the national leader in conversions. Approximately 70 projects with more than 20,000 potential units are underway, representing billions of dollars of investment and one of the fastest shifts in generations for how the city builds housing. Nate Bliss. Photo: Courtesy Latent Urban Ventures. Yet, remarkably little has been written about what is actually happening — or why. Over the past two years, office-to-residential conversions have gone from a trickle to a pour, and now represent a meaningful share of the multifamily pipeline. Thousands of units of affordable housing are being delivered in some of the wealthiest areas, including into buildings on Billionaires Row and Wall Street. Millions of square feet of defunct office space are being repurposed. And, despite a troubling week for the Pfizer building conversion, this trend is only accelerating. So how did New York City become the shining beacon for the office conversion revolution? Two game-changing moments occurred over the last two years. First, in April 2024, the governor and state legislature reached a landmark housing deal that included critical regulatory changes and a new incentive program called 467-m, designed to induce conversion projects and ensure that 25 percent of the resulting homes would be permanently affordable. The second moment came later that year, when the City Council passed City of Yes for Housing Opportunity, the most sweeping overhaul of zoning since the 1960s. For office conversions, City of Yes updated arcane rules around qualifying buildings and addressed regulatory incoherencies that had blocked past projects. What has resulted is one of the most effective housing production programs
bullets: NYC leads nation with ~70 office-to-residential conversion projects, 20,000+ potential units.
Billions in investment flowing into conversions; thousands of affordable units delivered in wealthy areas.
Conversions accelerating despite Pfizer building structural concerns; trend remains strong.
stories.summary: New York City has become the national leader in office-to-residential conversions, with approximately 70 projects representing over 20,000 potential units and billions in investment underway. The projects are delivering thousands of units of affordable housing in Manhattan's most expensive neighborhoods (Billionaires Row, Wall Street). Despite recent structural concerns at the Pfizer Building conversion, the overall conversion trend is accelerating and represents one of the fastest shifts in housing supply and typology in generations.
stories.impact: This is the single largest supply pipeline shift in NYC and a material headwind to new ground-up multifamily construction in Manhattan. Conversions offer faster delivery, lower hard-cost basis, and ready-made densities; they compete directly with new development for equity, debt, and absorption. Developers should recalibrate acquisition strategies in Manhattan—new ground-up projects in CBD/Midtown face converted-supply competition through 2027–2029. However, conversions are concentrated in low-income-restriction products (affordable housing); luxury/Class-A rents may remain insulated. Opportunity exists in non-conversion markets (outer boroughs, suburbs) and in submarkets where office stock is too new or functionally obsolete to convert economically.

stories.title: Charlotte Warehouse Investor Pays $41.4M for 4-Building Portfolio
stories.excerpt: Adler Real Estate Partners acquired four industrial assets in Arrowridge Business Park for $41.4 million. BankUnited provided a $25.6 million loan for the acquisition. Adler acquired 8041, 8107, 8227 and 8301 Arrowridge Blvd. in the transaction. All four buildings are about 48,600 square feet with 16-foot clear heights. The four buildings are 97% occupied. Cabot Properties was the seller. It acquired the Charlotte properties in 2021 from WHI Real Estate Partners and Childress Klein for $32.5 million. Arrowridge Business Park was originally developed by Childress Klein in 1979. It’s located off West Arrowood Road, near interstates 77 and 485. The park totals nine industrial properties with a combined 392,189 square feet across 13.6 acres. Childress Klein owns the five other buildings in the park. Dunn Mileham, David Morris and Eric Jennings of Trinity Partners represented Cabot Properties in the deal. Connect Industrial West | August 20 | Irvine, CAOn August 20, Connect Industrial West will bring together the owners, investors, developers, brokers, lenders, and occupiers driving industrial real estate across the Western U.S. Join top decision-makers for an afternoon of market insights, dealmaking opportunities, and networking with the industry’s most influential players.  Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post Charlotte Warehouse Investor Pays $41.4M for 4-Building Portfolio appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Adler RE acquired 4 Charlotte industrial buildings, 48.6k SF each, 97% occupied, for $41.4M.
BankUnited provided $25.6M loan (61.8% LTV); Cabot Properties seller (2021 acquisition).
Arrowridge Business Park (West Charlotte, I-77/485 junction) totals 392k SF, mixed ownership.
stories.summary: Adler Real Estate Partners purchased a four-building, 194,400-square-foot industrial portfolio in Charlotte's Arrowridge Business Park for $41.4 million. All four buildings are 97% occupied with 16-foot clear heights. BankUnited financed the deal with a $25.6 million loan. Seller Cabot Properties had acquired the assets in 2021 for $32.5 million, representing a gain despite a tight hold period.
stories.impact: Charlotte industrial market remains tight; 97% occupancy and positive price appreciation (2021–2026) signal strong fundamentals and tight supply. Arrowridge's proximity to I-77/485 and traditional warehouse tenant base demonstrates resilience outside of e-commerce/logistics hubs. For multifamily developers, strong industrial occupancy and investor competition signal healthy employment base and logistics clustering in Charlotte—positive for residential demand. Rising industrial cap rates and steady financing availability (BankUnited's engagement) suggest healthy credit conditions. Monitor whether industrial competition for land/capital diverts multifamily resources in Charlotte MSA.

stories.title: M&T Refis 300 East 50th Street Resi Tower With $141M Loan
stories.excerpt: A joint venture consisting of Global Holdings, MAG Partners and Safanad has sealed $141.4 million of bridge debt to refinance a newly built luxury apartment tower in Midtown Manhattan’s Turtle Bay neighborhood, Commercial Observer has learned M&T Realty Capital Corporation provided the loan on the sponsorship’s 194-unit Anagram Turtle Bay property at 300 East 50th Street. The deal closed nearly three years after MAG Partners and Safanad joined Eyal Ofer’s Global Holdings as joint venture partners for the project in late 2023 as part of a capitalization that involved Bank OZK supplying $95 million of construction financing. “Anagram Turtle Bay represents a premier multifamily asset backed by an outstanding sponsorship team,” Joe Pizzutelli, head of national production at M&T Realty Capital Corporation, said in a statement. The BFSK Architects-designed development features more than 142,000 square feet of residential space with apartment layouts ranging from one- to three-bedrooms. Community amenities include a rooftop terrace, a coworking lounge, a library and a fitness center. Anagram Turtle Bay also has nearly 5,000 square feet of ground-floor retail space leased to restaurant group Serafina Mare. “Our goal from day one was to create a residential community that would resonate with today’s renters while contributing to the continued evolution of the Midtown East neighborhood,” Josh Feder, chief investment officer at Global Holdings, said in a statement. “Achieving full lease-up so quickly and securing long-term financing speaks to the strength of that vision and demonstrates the enduring demand for thoughtfully designed, amenity-rich housing in Manhattan.” Global Holdings and MAG Partners also partnered in April 2026 to develop a multifamily tower at 122 Varick Street in Manhattan’s Hudson Square neighborhood. “Anagram Turtle Bay leased quickly because it resonated with our residents,” Jeff Rosen, managing principal and chief investm
bullets: M&T Realty refinanced 194-unit Anagram Turtle Bay (Manhattan) with $141.4M bridge loan.
JV of Global Holdings, MAG Partners, Safanad; newly built luxury tower, Midtown East.
Prior construction loan from Bank OZK $95M (2023); bridge indicates take-out/rate environment.
stories.summary: A joint venture of Global Holdings, MAG Partners, and Safanad secured a $141.4 million bridge refinance from M&T Realty Capital Corporation for Anagram Turtle Bay, a newly completed 194-unit luxury apartment tower in Midtown Manhattan (Turtle Bay/East 50th Street). The property was previously financed with $95 million in construction financing from Bank OZK in 2023. The bridge refinance signals transition from construction to stabilized operations.
stories.impact: Bridge financing on newly delivered Manhattan luxury product indicates capital markets appetite for prime NYC residential—lenders confident in exit/permanent financing or portfolio hold. However, bridge use (vs. permanent debt) suggests either tight permanent lending terms or sponsor intent to hold short-term and refinance as rates soften. For NYC multifamily developers: strong refinance availability for completed luxury assets validates construction underwriting. Turtle Bay remains prime submarket for Class-A rentals; comparable pipeline and delivery timelines should model similar bridge/permanent mix. Watch rate environment—if spreads tighten, competing projects will race to permanent debt and reduce bridge dependence.

stories.title: Marx Realty CEO Craig Deitelzweig Spied the Office-Hospitality Connection Early
stories.excerpt: When he isn’t home tending to his alpacas — yes, he has alpacas — Craig Deitelzweig, the chief executive officer of owner Marx Realty, is working out ways to beautify the company’s office portfolio, intent on making where people go to work a destination, not a chore. Deitelzweig, a former lawyer who graduated from Fordham University’s law school, became CEO of Marx Realty in 2017 and set out to reposition the firm’s portfolio, grow the company, and improve the value of the office properties in the key markets where the firm operates. Repositioning, Deitelzweig said, is an area where he developed a voracious appetite, relishing the process of turning something old into something new, which the firm has done with two of its crown jewels in Manhattan: 10 Grand Central and 545 Madison Avenue. And, while Marx Realty owns some of Manhattan’s most prominent office buildings, the firm’s portfolio also consists of properties in Washington, D.C., including the Herald at 1307 New York Avenue and the Grogan at 819 Seventh Street. Commercial Observer caught up with Deitelzweig in June to discuss what Marx Realty has been up to, and the importance of creating distinctive, hospitality-infused office spaces. This interview has been edited for length and clarity. Commercial Observer: What was your career journey from law to CEO of Marx Realty? Craig Deitelzweig: I started my career first in the litigation group and then in the real estate group at Skadden Arps. Then, from that firm I went to a private developer, and while at the developer I was initially in the legal and business side of things, but then, over time, I realized that I had this aptitude for repositioning office properties. First they put me on some properties that they had in Boston, which were sort of the redheaded stepchild of the company. They were just really troubled assets, and they sort of let me do my thing before I even knew I had a thing to do, and those repositionings were really successful. Even back then
bullets: Marx Realty CEO Craig Deitelzweig repositioning office portfolio; focus on 10 Grand Central, 545 Madison.
Strategy: convert offices into experiential, mixed-use destinations; holdings in NYC, Washington DC.
CEO emphasizes office-to-hospitality/amenity repositioning to drive value; moving beyond traditional office.
stories.summary: Marx Realty CEO Craig Deitelzweig is aggressively repositioning the company's office portfolio—including flagship properties 10 Grand Central and 545 Madison Avenue in Manhattan and the Herald and Grogan in Washington, D.C.—toward experiential mixed-use destinations that blend office with hospitality and amenity features. The strategy aims to make offices destinations rather than chores, reflecting the broader shift away from traditional office-only product.
stories.impact: Marx's strategy underscores sustained office-to-mixed-use/hospitality conversion thesis. While multifamily-specific conversions dominate headlines (Article 4), office-to-hospitality/experiential is an alternative use that competes for the same trophy buildings. For multifamily developers targeting high-barrier CBD sites in NYC and DC: premium office buildings with strong bones are being repositioned by sophisticated sponsors into high-margin experiential/hospitality rather than residential. This narrows the pool of convertible office stock suitable for multifamily and raises acquisition basis for remaining candidates. Implications: multifamily developers should prioritize older, lower-quality office stock (not trophy assets) for conversion strategies; trophy office towers will be taken by hospitality/experiential players with stronger exit comps.

stories.title: Stonemont Pursuing $70M Winder Warehouse Opportunity
stories.excerpt: Stonemont Financial Group is looking to build a 568,720-square-foot industrial facility in Winder. The Atlanta Business Chronicle reports the estimated value at build-out would be about $70 million. Winder is between Atlanta and Athens. Stonemount does not own the land. The Winder-Barrow Industrial Building Authority is the owner of record of the site, having taken title to it in June 2006. Stonemont’s development is expected to finish by the end of 2027. This proposal is located along Innovation Drive next to an existing 275,840-square-foot industrial building that was delivered in 2022. That facility is leased to Everluxe Building Products, which makes kitchen and bathroom cabinets. The site is also located near Lanier Technical College Winder-Barrow Campus, Innovation Amphitheater, and Barrow Arts & Sciences Academy. Stonemont co-developed the 7.7 million-square-foot Georgia International Trade Center near the Port of Savannah and several build-to-suit projects for Amazon. It has $5.3 billion in assets under management. The post Stonemont Pursuing $70M Winder Warehouse Opportunity appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Stonemont pursuing 568k SF industrial facility in Winder, Georgia (Atlanta–Athens corridor) valued ~$70M.
Delivery expected end of 2027; site owned by Winder-Barrow Industrial Authority; adjacent to existing 276k SF facility.
Stonemont manages $5.3B AUM; prior Amazon build-to-suit and Savannah port development experience.
stories.summary: Stonemont Financial Group is planning a 568,720-square-foot industrial facility in Winder, Georgia (between Atlanta and Athens) with an estimated build-out value of ~$70 million and completion targeted for end of 2027. The site is located along Innovation Drive and sits adjacent to an existing 2022-delivered 275,840-square-foot facility leased to Everluxe Building Products. The Winder-Barrow Industrial Authority owns the land.
stories.impact: Winder is an emerging secondary logistics market between major metros; Stonemont's investment signals confidence in Georgia's logistics corridor expansion beyond core Atlanta/Savannah. However, limited multifamily relevance directly. Indirectly: industrial clusters and logistics employment drive population migration and wage growth in secondary markets; Winder's emergence as an industrial hub could support future multifamily demand in the Athens/Madison/North Georgia corridor. Developers should monitor Winder for residential spillover if industrial employment concentrates. Stonemont's developer sophistication and track record (Amazon, Savannah) validates Winder's institutional investment grade—positive for overall market stability.

stories.title: OpenSpace CEO Jeevan Kalanithi: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: Not many proptech companies have been able to scale their technology to take advantage of the data center construction boom, but one that has cracked the code in servicing the sector’s gargantuan growth is OpenSpace. The San Francisco-based visual intelligence platform has worked on more than 1,000 data center projects across the U.S., Europe, the Middle East, Canada, Australia and Singapore. Overall, contractors and owners have used the platform to document and analyze progress on more than 100,000 construction projects globally. Jeevan Kalanithi, CEO and co-founder of OpenSpace, spoke with Commercial Observer last week about what he sees as his company’s unique view into one of the biggest and least discussed challenges facing artificial intelligence: physically building the infrastructure required to support it. Kalanithi also discussed why the AI race has entered a physical phase, the biggest construction challenges emerging across hyperscale and AI-focused projects, why real-time visibility is becoming critical to managing risk and schedule certainty, and what owners, contractors and developers are learning as AI infrastructure scales globally. This interview has been edited for length and clarity. Commercial Observer: OpenSpace has worked with about 1,000 data centers globally. What does your company bring to servicing these unique facilities? Jeevan Kalanithi: I think there’s two main narratives there. We just make it far easier and less risky to build out anything. Of course, we’re experiencing a boom in data centers, and our technology is super well tuned for taking the risk out of building those projects. The second thing we’re finding is that some of the latest advances in our technology and offerings are extremely well tuned for data, specifically what we call OpenSpace Track, which is our progress-tracking system. This is the ability to take information from all kinds of data sources — 360-degree cameras, mobile phones, etc. — which we
bullets: OpenSpace CEO Jeevan Kalanithi: proptech platform scaled to 1,000+ data center projects globally.
100k+ total construction projects tracked; platform critical to AI infrastructure build-out visibility.
Data center construction boom seen as physical phase of AI race; project management/risk critical.
stories.summary: OpenSpace, a San Francisco-based visual intelligence construction platform, has scaled to manage over 1,000 data center projects globally and track 100,000+ construction projects overall. CEO Jeevan Kalanithi discusses the company's unique vantage into hyperscale AI infrastructure construction, describing the boom as the 'physical phase of the AI race.' The platform provides real-time visibility, schedule management, and risk mitigation for massive, complex builds.
stories.impact: Indirect but material for multifamily: massive capital reallocation toward data center construction and AI infrastructure is diverting construction labor, materials, and financing from residential development. Data centers command premium pricing (higher returns on capital) and attract top-tier general contractors and supply chains. In competitive labor markets (West Coast, Austin, North Texas), multifamily developers face headwinds on GC availability and labor costs due to data center prioritization. Secondary effect: data center clusters in Austin, Northern Virginia, Phoenix, and other metros drive population influx but also construction inflation. Multifamily developers should budget for elevated labor/materials costs and longer GC procurement timelines through 2026–2028; consider counter-cyclical markets less affected by data center construction booms.

stories.title: South Florida’s top deals: Telecom exec pays $19M for new Miami mansion
stories.excerpt: 🏆 Residential: The priciest home sale to hit records in South Florida was in Miami’s Ponce-Davis neighborhood. An LLC managed by Christophe Scherman parted with the roughly 10,000-square-foot mansion at 5545 Southwest 81st Terrace for $18.9 million. An entity led by telecommunications executive Meinrad Spenger bought the house. Built this year, the home sits on a nearly 1-acre lot and has seven bedrooms and seven and a half bathrooms. It also has a separate guest house. David Siddons with Douglas Elliman represented the seller, and Wesley Ulloa and Christopher Borchetta with Luxe Properties brought the buyer. The home’s most recent […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami mansion sold for $18.9M (telecom executive buyer); Wynwood commercial totals $24.5M (developer Moishe Mana).
South Florida luxury residential and mixed-use/creative commercial remain active; strong investor interest.
Jupiter waterfront and Miami neighborhoods show continued high-price appreciation and investor mobility.
stories.summary: South Florida's top July 2026 deals include a $18.9 million luxury mansion sale in Miami's Ponce-Davis neighborhood (telecom executive buyer, 10,000 SF, built 2026) and a $24.5 million commercial acquisition in Wynwood (13,000 SF mixed-use, purchased by developer Moishe Mana). Additional activity includes a $10.5 million waterfront home sale in Jupiter. Markets show strong investor and end-user demand across residential and creative commercial segments.
stories.impact: Minimal direct development impact. However, persistent luxury-home appreciation and Mana's continued Wynwood accumulation signal South Florida's resilience as a wealth destination and creative-economy hub. Moishe Mana's portfolio play (acquiring trophy creative real estate) suggests confidence in South Florida's creative-class retention and rental/experiential upside. For multifamily: strong wealth migration into South Florida (telecom/tech executives) validates demand fundamentals. Mana's strategy (creative mixed-use, not traditional multifamily) shows alternative operators are claiming prime real estate; multifamily developers should secure land ahead of creative/hospitality conversion wave. South Florida multifamily still undersupplied relative to migration; supply pipeline remains opportunity.

stories.title: Experiential Retail: What Works Now and What Doesn’t
stories.excerpt: Area 15 in Las Vegas is a retail and entertainment complex featuring shops and food and beverage outlets in addition to attractions that take the complex far beyond traditional retail. Inside Area 15, which New York-based Fisher Brothers developed, Interstellar Arc bills itself as “the world’s largest VR experience.” Speaking of experiences, the John Wick Experience takes you inside the beloved Keanu Reeves film and challenges you to escape, with a full bar awaiting you once you do. Meow Wolf’s Omega Mart, meanwhile, is a psychedelic supermarket of sorts, presenting shelves filled with inconceivable products — “Who Told You This Was Butter?” home freshening spray, anyone? — carrying descriptions seemingly penned in another dimension. According to market research firm Metatech Insights, the global experiential retail market stood at $132 billion in 2025, but is projected to “more than quadruple” by 2035, with an expected compound annual growth rate of around 15.2 percent throughout that decade. The reasons for the popularity of experiential retail — which Metatech describes as “a type of strategy in which physical retail locations are being crafted to provide clients with interesting, immersive and memorable shopping experiences, as opposed to exchanges of product” — are largely related to changing consumer desires among younger generations. Metatech notes in its report that experiential retail is driven by a consumer desire for “an exhilarating shopping reality,” saying that “the current clientele, especially the millennials and Gen Z, want meaningful, emotional and interactive shopping experiences which will resonate with their ideals and ways of living.” “Retail, by definition, started as purely transactional,” said Tyler Winograd, retail and consumer experience studio director at architecture firm Gensler. “It was about creating space where you walked in, purchased the most likely product in exchange for money, and walked out. There was nothing happening within t
bullets: Global experiential retail market $132B in 2025; projected to exceed $528B by 2035 (15.2% CAGR).
Area 15 (Las Vegas), Meow Wolf, VR experiences anchoring retail; blending retail, entertainment, F&B.
Experiential model moving beyond malls; integrated, Instagram-worthy, all-ages destinations.
stories.summary: According to Metatech Insights, the global experiential retail market reached $132 billion in 2025 and is projected to exceed $528 billion by 2035, a compound annual growth rate of approximately 15.2%. Examples include Area 15 in Las Vegas (VR experiences, Meow Wolf's Omega Mart, John Wick Experience) and similar immersive retail environments that blend shopping, dining, and entertainment into destination experiences rather than transactional retail.
stories.impact: Experiential retail growth is a headwind for traditional retail and an alternative use for mixed-use development sites. Multifamily developers pursuing mixed-use or ground-floor retail should anticipate: (1) higher tenant expectations for experiential/entertainment components, pushing up TI and site infrastructure costs; (2) competition for location from pure-play experiential operators with venture backing; (3) need to design for flexibility and high-touch F&B/entertainment, not commodity retail. Positive: experiential retail drives foot traffic and dwell time, which can anchor multifamily projects if co-located. Strategy: integrate smaller experiential anchors (boutique VR, immersive dining) into ground floors vs. competing with mega-players. Watch for experiential operators seeking multifamily partnerships to cross-promote residents as customers.

stories.title: Elysian, Capstone go 100% affordable for Warner Center apartment project
stories.excerpt: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities are planning to build a new affordable housing development in Woodland Hills.  Los Angeles-based Elysian Housing and New York-based Capstone Equities purchased 21155 Califa Street, L.A. Business First reported. A one-story, 35,000-square-foot commercial building at the site would be demolished to make way for more than 300 units of income-restricted housing. Terms of the transaction, including price, were not disclosed.  Elysian and Capstone plan to erect 316 affordable units across seven stories on the 2.7 acre property. The proposed project is tied to the largest new construction permit of the first week of this month, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Elysian Housing, Capstone Equities building 316 affordable units in Woodland Hills (Warner Center), LA.
100% affordable; 7-story, 300k SF; expedited approval via LA Executive Directive 1.
Project tied to Stan Kroenke's 52-acre mixed-use development (LA Rams HQ); neighborhood transitioning to affordable housing.
stories.summary: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities are developing The Green at Warner Center in Los Angeles's Woodland Hills neighborhood, a 316-unit 100% affordable multifamily project at 21155 Califa Street. The seven-story, 300,020-square-foot building replaces a 35,000-square-foot commercial structure and qualifies for expedited approval under LA's Executive Directive 1. The project is part of a broader transformation of Warner Center, adjacent to Stan Kroenke's $1B+ mixed-use Rams headquarters development (52 acres).
stories.impact: Warner Center is emerging as an affordable-housing density hub amid luxury mixed-use redevelopment (Kroenke's Rams campus). Elysian/Capstone's strategy—converting/replacing non-residential real estate with 100% affordable multifamily—is repeatable across LA and signals that affordable operators are acquiring trophy sites ahead of market appreciation. For market-rate multifamily developers: this creates a bifurcated Warner Center—luxury mixed-use (Kroenke) anchoring wealth, but nearby 100% affordable housing absorbing workforce. Implication: market-rate multifamily upside in Warner Center remains strong (rents supported by luxury district), but competition from affordable supply and public subsidy means capture rates lower than adjacent areas. Developers should focus on transit-adjacent market-rate product outside Warner Center or premium service apartments (luxury rentals) that differentiate from affordable. Monitor LA's expedited approval pipeline—more 100% affordable starts could depress market-rate financing and returns.

stories.title: Austin suburb selects Griffin Swinerton to revamp downtown
stories.excerpt: One of the nation’s fastest growing suburbs is enlisting a new developer to bring its downtown space up to code as it welcomes in a steady influx of new residents.  Liberty Hill’s city officials selected San Francisco-based Griffin Swinerton, a joint venture of Griffin Structures Incorporated and builder Swinerton Incorporated to head up the downtown facelift. The joint venture will officially function as though they were city staff, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The responsibility extends beyond listening to what the community wants and then delivering. Griffin Swinerton will be tasked with actively soliciting community thoughts as well as […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Liberty Hill, Texas (Austin suburb) selects Griffin Swinerton for downtown revamp; target start 2028.
Fast-growing suburb (tripled population since 2020 census) needs modernized downtown infrastructure.
Developer tasked with entitlement, parking, walkability, historic preservation, community engagement, financing.
stories.summary: Liberty Hill, an Austin suburb that has tripled in population since 2020, selected San Francisco-based Griffin Swinerton (JV of Griffin Structures and Swinerton) to lead a downtown transformation targeting a 2028 start. The joint venture will serve as an extension of city staff, handling entitlement, infrastructure needs, financing evaluation, community input, historic preservation, and design of walkable, multi-use districts with upgraded parking and industry-specific zones.
stories.impact: Liberty Hill's explosive population growth (tripling since 2020) is a strong demand signal for both residential and mixed-use. Griffin Swinerton's selection to lead downtown revamp indicates the city recognizes bottlenecks in housing/retail/service supply relative to population influx. Multifamily developers should view Liberty Hill as an upstream opportunity: downtown revamp will catalyze property values and rental fundamentals, creating arbitrage for early entrants (pre-downtown finalization). City's prioritization of walkability and community gathering suggests receptivity to higher-density multifamily near downtown core. Nearby suburbs (Pflugerville) are already seeing major mixed-use pipelines (Downtown East, 29 acres); Liberty Hill likely follows. First-mover advantage for multifamily in Liberty Hill before downtown transformation is complete; coordinate with Griffin Swinerton's timeline for land acquisition and zoning.

stories.title: CMK grabs key permit to launch 28-story South Loop high-rise
stories.excerpt: A planned South Loop high-rise is all but ready for shovels to break ground. The CMK Companies’ planned mixed-use development at 910 South Wells just snagged its piles permit, and work on the foundation can commence.  CMK already received site plan approval last November, according to Urbanize Chicago. Gensler is heading up the design for the 28-story building, which will feature 3,300 square feet of space for retail and 350 residential units. Plans also include ample parking space, an outdoor amenity space for residents and a riverwalk view. The building will be connected to the riverwalk using a pedestrian path. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: CMK Companies' 910 South Wells (South Loop, Chicago) secured piles permit for 28-story high-rise.
350 residential units, 3,300 SF retail, parking, riverwalk connection; Gensler design.
Part of CMK's truncated Riverline master plan (8 acres, $2B original vision); construction loan $83M (Wintrust).
stories.summary: CMK Companies' 910 South Wells Street in Chicago's South Loop obtained its piles permit, clearing the path for foundation work on a 28-story mixed-use high-rise designed by Gensler. The building will contain 350 residential units, 3,300 square feet of ground-floor retail, parking, and resident amenities, with a pedestrian riverwalk connection. The project is part of CMK's Riverline master plan (eight acres; originally $2 billion with Lendlease before 2018 split). CMK secured an $83 million construction loan from Wintrust Bank for the first phase, which includes a 388-unit apartment tower at 1010 South Wells (already under construction).
stories.impact: CMK is executing a phased South Loop infill strategy with two concurrent towers (1010 and 910 South Wells) targeting 2027–2029 delivery. Strong permitting velocity and lender confidence (Wintrust's $83M construction loan) validate Chicago South Loop fundamentals. For developers: CMK's approach (phased, walkable, mixed-use, riverfront-adjacent) is the institutional blueprint for dense urban infill. South Loop's supply pipeline remains healthy; competition for renters among CMK and other operators (Lendlease's completed Cooper and Reed towers) will intensify rents in 2027–2029. Multifamily developers should monitor South Loop leasing velocity—if 1010 South Wells absorbs faster than forecast, rents will accelerate and justify higher basis for competing projects. Chicago's low cost vs. coastal markets makes it attractive for new supply; developers should accelerate acquisition if South Loop rents trend up.

stories.title: Therme Dallas pivots, adds skybridge to $800M wellness resort buildout plans
stories.excerpt: Therme Dallas is looking for city approval to add a pedestrian skybridge and new non-residential zones to their $800 million resort development.  The Austrian wellness company is looking to break ground on the 24-acre, 500,000-square-foot resort in 2027. Plans for the massive resort on 1500 South Riverfront Boulevard include swim-up bars, thermal baths, saunas and restaurants, all within a beach-like atmosphere with a retractable glass roof. The skybridge will connect two of the planned buildings, according to the Dallas Business Journal, but which two is unclear.  Therme raised eyebrows around a year ago when it purchased the land for the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Therme Dallas $800M wellness resort (24 acres, 500k SF) targeting 2027 groundbreaking.
Proposed skybridge addition; thermal baths, saunas, swim-up bars, retractable glass roof.
Company facing credibility questions: misrepresented development experience to Ontario regulators; only one built facility (Bucharest).
stories.summary: Therme Dallas, an Austrian wellness company, is seeking Dallas city approval to add a pedestrian skybridge and non-residential zones to its planned $800 million resort on the Trinity River (24 acres, 500,000 SF). The project aims for 2027 groundbreaking and will feature thermal baths, saunas, swim-up bars, and restaurants under a retractable glass roof. However, a New York Times investigation revealed Therme misrepresented its development experience to Ontario; the company has only successfully built one spa (Bucharest) and misattributed other facilities to itself.
stories.impact: Therme Dallas is a major speculative gateway project for Dallas hospitality/experiential real estate. Credibility concerns around developer execution capacity create uncertainty about timing and completion probability. For multifamily developers: if Therme stalls or defaults, it could dampen Trinity River corridor investor confidence and delay ancillary multifamily projects planned around the resort. Alternatively, if Therme succeeds, it anchors experiential retail/hospitality in Dallas and drives residential demand in nearby neighborhoods (potential upside for nearby multifamily). Recommend monitoring Therme's financing announcements and construction starts closely; any delays/defaults could signal broader financing stress in experiential real estate. Secondary effect: Therme's resort model competes with hospitality for capital and credit; tightening of hospitality financing could also strain multifamily debt markets. Track closely.

stories.title: Tannos secures $28M in construction financing for Houston area mixed-use project
stories.excerpt: Tannos Development Groups’ Friendswood mixed-use project is one step closer to coming to fruition.  The Friendswood-based developer secured $28 million in mid-construction financing for The Albritton, a mixed-use development being built at 408 South Friendswood Drive, according to a release from Miami-based Concord Summit Capital, which arranged the deal.  Concord Summit’s Daniel J. Eidson and Ben Applebaum sourced the financing. The debt, which has a 70 percent loan-to-value ratio, is supposed to carry the project “through the remainder of construction,” Eidson said in the release. S3 Capital provided the financing, Commercial Observer reported.  The Albritton will feature 111 multifamily units, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Tannos Development secured $28M mid-construction financing for The Albritton (Friendswood, Houston area).
111 multifamily units, 11k SF retail, 9k SF office; completion 2028; 70% LTV, S3 Capital lender.
Tannos also building Friendswood City Center ($750M, 106 acres) and Magnolia Town Center ($1B, 200 acres, partner Maritia).
stories.summary: Tannos Development Group secured a $28 million mid-construction financing facility from S3 Capital for The Albritton, a mixed-use project in Friendswood (25 miles southeast of Houston, halfway between Houston and Galveston). The project will deliver 111 multifamily units, 11,000 square feet of retail, and 9,000 square feet of office space by 2028. The loan carries a 70% LTV ratio. Tannos is simultaneously developing two larger projects: Friendswood City Center ($750 million, 106 acres, construction started Spring 2024) and Magnolia Town Center ($1 billion, 200 acres, partner Maritia).
stories.impact: Tannos is aggregating development momentum in Houston's outer suburbs (Friendswood, Magnolia), capturing exurban growth and positioning for long-term appreciation. Mid-construction refinancing availability (70% LTV, $28M) signals lender confidence in Houston metro fundamentals and Tannos's execution track record. Three concurrent projects totaling ~$1.78B in gross value indicates robust capital availability for suburban Houston mixed-use. For multifamily developers: Tannos's pace and scale show Houston outer suburbs (Friendswood, Magnolia) are now institutional-grade development markets. Land prices and financing terms are tightening; early movers with entitlements will capture best economics. Opportunity: smaller markets between Houston and Galveston (Pearland, League City, Texas City) likely next wave of out-migration; developers should acquire ahead of mega-developer saturation. Monitor Friendswood absorption rates (The Albritton, City Center) for demand signal.
